metadata.purpose: Morels and other cup, saddle, and ascomycete-type records.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes:
Spring morels with darker ridges and a fully attached cap; hollow from tip through stem.

Image repair note: v4.3.116 replaced older Special:FilePath black-morel images with direct upload.wikimedia Commons images after live cards/details showed no images.
records.culinary_uses: Choice spring edible; always cook thoroughly. Slice lengthwise to confirm a hollow interior and to clean out grit or insects.
records.general_notes: Grouped here as a practical field-guide complex rather than a single strict species concept.
records.edibility_detail: Excellent edible; false morels are the main danger.

mushroom_profile.spore_print: Pale cream to ochraceous deposit may occur; spores are produced in asci in the pitted cap surface, and a print is not the practical field identification test.
fertile_surface: Asci lining the cap pits; ridges largely sterile
mushroom_profile.season_note: Primarily a spring mushroom, often during leaf-out and warming soil.

records.field_identification: True morel with pitted cap, hollow interior from cap through stem, and cap attached to the stem; darkening ridges in black morels.
records.mushroom_card_note: Prime spring mushroom; confirm hollow true-morel structure and cook thoroughly.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Morchella angusticeps
use_links.notes: Black morel traits, hollow interior, attachment, and comparison notes.

records.look_alike_notes: False morels are the danger: true morels are hollow from cap through stem and have a pitted attached cap; Gyromitra types are chambered/cottony/wrinkled rather than cleanly hollow. Every morel must be cut lengthwise before eating; discard any specimen that is not hollow from cap tip to stem base or has a wrinkled/brain-like cap instead of true pits. Link false morels/Gyromitra and Verpa-style early false morels as primary dangers.
records.quality_notes: Excellent spring mushroom when fresh and firm; quality drops when old, dry, buggy, sandy, or mushy.
records.eating_use_notes: Clean well, cut lengthwise, inspect each specimen, and cook thoroughly. Try a small first serving.
records.risk_reason: False morels and Verpa-type look-alikes can be poisonous. Even true morels can cause gastrointestinal upset if raw, undercooked, eaten in large amounts, or possibly combined with alcohol.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If confused with false morels: gastrointestinal tract, liver, kidneys, nervous system, and blood. If true morels are raw/undercooked: gastrointestinal upset.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Morel-like mushrooms may be wrinkled, lobed, chambered, or attached differently. True morels should be hollow from cap tip through stem when cut lengthwise, with the cap properly attached to the stem.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat morels raw or undercooked. Do not eat any “morel” unless every specimen is cut lengthwise and confirmed as true Morchella.
records.caution_reason: False morels and Verpa-type look-alikes can be poisonous. Even true morels can cause gastrointestinal upset if raw, undercooked, eaten in large amounts, or possibly combined with alcohol.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — false morel / Verpa confusion possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: Extreme — false morel / Verpa confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: True morels must be cut lengthwise; false morels and Verpa can overlap in spring.
required_checks: Cut every specimen lengthwise
Interior must be hollow from cap through stem
Cap must be properly attached for the target morel type
Reject brain-like folds, saddle shapes, cottony/chambered interiors, or top-attached caps
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Every morel goes under the knife. If it is not cleanly hollow with the right cap attachment, do not eat it.

target_traits: Cap attachment matches true morel type
lookalike_traits: Brain-like, wrinkled, lobed, or saddle-like cap
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Non-hollow or brain-like morel-ish mushrooms are not edible morels.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Not edible / early false morel caution.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Top-attached free-hanging cap means Verpa caution, not true morel.

records.source_note: v4.3.209 clarifies ascospore production and keeps print color secondary to true-morel structure; exact complex separation may require microscopy or DNA.

records.notes: Cap hangs partly free from the stem rather than being attached all the way down.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Cook like other true morels after cleaning and lengthwise confirmation. Do not eat raw.
records.general_notes: Half-free morels are real morels, but their cap attachment makes beginners nervous. The hollow interior and true pitted cap are the key checks.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible after confident true-morel identification. Slice lengthwise to confirm hollow interior and cap attachment; avoid any brainy, chambered, or cottony false morels.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring morel season, usually May in Upper Michigan.
fertile_surface: Asci lining the cap pits; ridges largely sterile
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Yellowish-orange to whitish deposit may occur; it is not a primary field-safety test.

records.field_identification: Spring morel with a cap attached only partway down the stem, leaving the lower cap edge hanging free like a skirt. Cap has true pits and ridges, and the mushroom is hollow from cap tip through stem when sliced lengthwise.
records.mushroom_card_note: Spring true morel with a skirt-like half-free cap; slice lengthwise before trusting it.

use_links.label: MSU Extension: May is Morel Month
use_links.notes: Michigan-oriented morel safety and harvest context.

edible_use.notes: Good edible after confident true-morel identification. Slice lengthwise to confirm hollow interior and cap attachment; avoid any brainy, chambered, or cottony false morels.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — false morel / Verpa confusion possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: Extreme — false morel / Verpa confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Half-free morels require a special attachment check because the lower cap edge hangs free, but the cap is not attached only at the top like Verpa.
required_checks: Cut every specimen lengthwise
Interior must be hollow from cap through stem
Cap attached about halfway down the stem
Reject top-only attached caps, cottony interiors, chambered interiors, brain-like folds, saddle shapes, or non-hollow specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Half-free morels must be hollow and attached partway down the stem; top-only attached caps are Verpa caution.

target_traits: Cap attachment matches true morel type
lookalike_traits: Brain-like, wrinkled, lobed, or saddle-like cap
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Non-hollow or brain-like morel-ish mushrooms are not edible morels.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Not edible / early false morel caution.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Top-attached free-hanging cap means Verpa caution, not true morel.

records.source_note: v4.3.209 adds ascospore-deposit context while keeping cap attachment and a cleanly hollow interior as the required field checks.

records.display_name: Yellow morels — see White / yellow morels
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Choice spring edible when confirmed as a true morel. Clean well, slice lengthwise, and cook thoroughly; do not eat raw or undercooked.
records.general_notes: Representative Commons images currently use Morchella esculenta / yellow-morel-type material for this field-guide group record.
records.look_alike_notes: Every morel must be cut lengthwise before eating; discard any specimen that is not hollow from cap tip to stem base or has a wrinkled/brain-like cap instead of true pits. Link false morels/Gyromitra and Verpa-style early false morels as primary dangers.

records.caution_reason: False morels and Verpa-type look-alikes can be poisonous. Even true morels can cause gastrointestinal upset if raw, undercooked, eaten in large amounts, or possibly combined with alcohol.

records.redirect_note: Yellow morels are covered under the canonical white-yellow-morels record.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — false morel / Verpa confusion possible

records.culinary_uses: Cook true morels thoroughly. Good sautéed, added to sauces, dried for later use, or used in soups; never eat raw morels.
records.edibility_detail: True morels are edible only after confident ID and cooking. Confirm a hollow interior, attached cap, and pitted honeycomb surface; avoid false morels and half-free look-alikes unless separately identified.
records.edibility_notes: Michigan DNR treats white/common morel as edible and notes it fruits in the latter half of May. All wild mushrooms should be cleaned and well-cooked before consuming.

stem_feature: Cap attached to stalk at base
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late May into early June in northern Michigan; Michigan DNR says white/common morels fruit in the latter half of May.
fertile_surface: Asci lining the cap pits; ridges largely sterile
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Pale cream to yellowish deposit may occur, but conventional printing is not a practical true-morel safety test.

records.field_identification: True white/yellow morels have a pitted honeycomb cap with ridges and pits, the cap is attached to the stem for most or all of its length, and the mushroom is hollow from cap tip through stem when sliced lengthwise.
records.mushroom_card_note: True morels: hollow inside, pitted cap, cap attached to stem; false morels are the stop sign.
records.look_alike_notes: False morels and Verpa species can fruit in the same season. Cut lengthwise: true morels are hollow and pitted, while false morels may be wrinkled, wavy, brain-like, free-hanging, solid, or chambered. Every morel must be cut lengthwise before eating; discard any specimen that is not hollow from cap tip to stem base or has a wrinkled/brain-like cap instead of true pits. Link false morels/Gyromitra and Verpa-style early false morels as primary dangers.

edible_use.notes: Clean, split lengthwise, inspect, and cook thoroughly before eating.

use_links.notes: Michigan true morel traits, white/common morel timing, false-morel cautions, and cooking reminder.

records.culinary_uses: Edible after confident true-morel ID and thorough cooking; clean carefully because burn-site morels may hold ash, grit, and insects in pits.
records.edibility_detail: Edible true morel only after confident true-morel identification and thorough cooking; avoid if false-morel traits are present or the specimen is deteriorated.

stem_feature: Cap attached to stem as a true morel
mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring, especially the first year after fire; sometimes reported in the second spring after a burn.
fertile_surface: Asci lining the pitted cap surface; no gills or pores
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Not useful for casual field identification; spores are produced in asci in the pitted cap surface, and exact burn-morel species may require microscopic or molecular work.

records.field_identification: Fire-associated true morel: hollow from cap tip through stem, pitted cap with darker ridges at maturity, no cottony/chambered interior like false morels, and a strong recent-burn context. Search especially in the first spring after a conifer/pine burn; in Michigan this is a northern spring morel and the burn history, hollow body, pitted cap, and season should all agree before considering it.
records.mushroom_card_note: Localized true morel of recent burn sites; clean off ash and cook thoroughly.
records.look_alike_notes: False morels may occur in spring. Confirm hollow true-morel structure and pitted cap; avoid brain-like, wrinkled, chambered, solid, or free-hanging forms. Every morel must be cut lengthwise before eating; discard any specimen that is not hollow from cap tip to stem base or has a wrinkled/brain-like cap instead of true pits. Link false morels/Gyromitra and Verpa-style early false morels as primary dangers.

edible_use.notes: Clean carefully, remove ash residue, split lengthwise, inspect, and cook thoroughly before eating.

use_links.notes: Burn-site morel notes, true morel traits, false-morel cautions, and ash-residue reminder.

use_links.notes: Michigan burn-site morel habitat and Mi-Morels map context.

field_measurements.notes: Definitive published cm/inch cap-and-stem range for Morchella exuberans was not verified in this pass; do not infer size from other morels.
field_measurements.missing_measurement_reason: Accessible references confirmed burn-site ecology and true-morel characters, but did not provide a clean species-specific size range.

completion_gaps: Definitive species-specific cap/stem/body measurements in cm and inches were not verified from accessible sources in this pass.
